channel.title: Nature Materials
channel.description: Nature Materials is a multidisciplinary journal aimed at bringing together cutting-edge research across the entire spectrum of materials science and technology. Nature Materials covers all applied and fundamental aspects of the synthesis/processing, structure/composition, properties and performance of materials. Nature Materials provides a forum for the development of a common identity among materials scientists while encouraging researchers to cross established subdisciplinary lines. To achieve this, Nature Materials takes an interdisciplinary, integrated and balanced approach to all areas of materials research while fostering the exchange of ideas between scientists involved in different communities.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Deeper insights into charge photogeneration in organic solar cells
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 27 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02500-1

An advanced model is developed to pinpoint the parameters that control the bulk charge photogeneration yield and to provide design strategies for highly efficient organic solar cells.
item.dc:title: Deeper insights into charge photogeneration in organic solar cells
item.dc:creator: Carsten Deibel

item.title: Molecular factors controlling charge pair generation in organic photovoltaic materials
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 27 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02509-6

A model is devised to investigate free charge generation in neat acceptor domains and at donor–acceptor heterojunctions. Efficient photocurrent generation in organic heterojunctions at low energetic offsets can be related to key molecular parameters and to electronic state delocalization.
item.dc:title: Molecular factors controlling charge pair generation in organic photovoltaic materials
item.dc:creator: Lucy J. F. Hart
Daniel G. Medranda
Shi Wei Yuan
Linnea Lindh
Jolanda S. Müller
Hanbo Yang
Hugo Gerard
Tianyu Zhao
Arianna Quesada-Ramirez
Mariano Campoy-Quiles
Mohammed Azzouzi
Flurin D. Eisner
Jenny Nelson

item.title: Engineering phase-frustration-induced flat bands in an aza-triangulene covalent kagome lattice
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 27 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02528-3

The on-surface synthesis of π-conjugated covalent organic frameworks (COFs) enables the realization of designer quantum nanomaterials. Orbital-phase frustration within an aza-triangulene COF-based kagome lattice is reported to give rise to non-trivial flat bands.
item.dc:title: Engineering phase-frustration-induced flat bands in an aza-triangulene covalent kagome lattice
item.dc:creator: Yuyi Yan
Fujia Liu
Weichen Tang
Han Xuan Wong
Boyu Qie
Steven G. Louie
Felix R. Fischer

item.title: A modern perspective on antiferroelectrics
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 27 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02483-z

Antiferroelectrics possess an antipolar ground state, and can be electrically driven into a polar phase. This Perspective discusses the history of these systems, defines these materials, surveys different types of antiferroelectricity and presents an outlook on future research pathways.
item.dc:title: A modern perspective on antiferroelectrics
item.dc:creator: Gustau Catalan
Alexei Gruverman
Jorge Íñiguez-González
Dennis Meier
Morgan Trassin

item.title: Key role of oxidizing species driving water oxidation revealed by time-resolved optical and X-ray spectroscopies
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 26 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02514-9

Iridium oxide is a state-of-the-art catalyst for green hydrogen production, but the identity of species present under operating conditions remains debated. Here, using multiple operando spectroscopy approaches with time-resolution, water oxidation is found to be driven by O−1 states.
item.dc:title: Key role of oxidizing species driving water oxidation revealed by time-resolved optical and X-ray spectroscopies
item.dc:creator: Caiwu Liang
Lucas Garcia Verga
Benjamin Moss
Santosh Kumar
Soren B. Scott
Mark A. Turner
Pilar Ferrer
Veronica Celorrio
Dave C. Grinter
Yemin Tao
Sid Halder
Yifeng Wang
Cindy Tseng
Guangmeimei Yang
Georg Held
Sarah J. Haigh
Aron Walsh
Ifan E. L. Stephens
James R. Durrant
Reshma R. Rao

item.title: How ions squeeze water out of n-type ladder organic mixed conductors
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02490-0

Unlike common organic mixed ionic–electronic conductors that swell upon electrochemical doping, poly(benzimidazobenzophenanthroline) exhibits an unexpected cation-dependent deswelling at high doping levels. Operando measurements and modelling show that protic cations drive this response by expelling water and localizing charge through strong interactions with the polymer backbone, revealing a distinct deswelling mechanism.
item.dc:title: How ions squeeze water out of n-type ladder organic mixed conductors

item.title: Topological control of spontaneous failure in active nematic solids
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02493-x

Topological defect quadrupoles orchestrate predictable failure patterns in active nematic solid networks.
item.dc:title: Topological control of spontaneous failure in active nematic solids
item.dc:creator: Sheng Chen
Matthew Ricci
A. Pasha Tabatabai
Zachary Gao Sun
Sven Witthaus
Suraj Shankar
Mor Nitzan
Michael P. Murrell

item.title: An ångström-scale Janus aperture as a gas flow rectifier
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02513-w

Gas transport through an ångström-sized Janus aperture in graphene, which is created by ozone etching, is investigated by using high-resolution transport measurements and high-throughput atomistic simulations. Rectified gas flow for various gases is demonstrated with rectification ratios of up to two orders of magnitude for oxygen.
item.dc:title: An ångström-scale Janus aperture as a gas flow rectifier
item.dc:creator: Hongwei Duan
Jing Yang
Nianjie Liang
Xiaobo Chen
Shengping Zhang
Anshul Saxena
Zeyu Zhuang
Ruiyang Song
Junhe Tong
Kaihui Liu

Luda Wang
